title: Cardiac Arrest: Asystole / PEA
learningObjectives: Describe the immediate management sequence for asystole and pulseless electrical activity (PEA).
Explain why defibrillation is NOT indicated for asystole or PEA.
State the correct epinephrine dose, concentration, and timing for nonshockable rhythms.
List and explain the H's and T's as reversible causes of cardiac arrest.
Differentiate the PEA subtypes and explain how they may guide treatment.
Describe how early epinephrine timing (within 3–5 minutes) is prioritized for nonshockable rhythms.
Explain the role of waveform capnography in confirming true asystole and monitoring CPR quality.
Describe when mechanical CPR devices may be considered.

summary.title: Defining Asystole and PEA
summary.content: Asystole is the complete absence of ventricular electrical activity — a flatline on the ECG with no pulse. Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) is any organized electrical rhythm (normal or abnormal) on the ECG that fails to generate a palpable pulse. Both are nonshockable rhythms — the cornerstone of management is high-quality CPR and early identification and treatment of reversible causes, rather than defibrillation.

summary.title: The Asystole/PEA Algorithm
summary.content: Upon identifying asystole or PEA, the team immediately begins high-quality chest compressions and ventilations. Epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO (1:10,000 concentration) is given as soon as feasible, ideally within 3–5 minutes of arrest onset for nonshockable rhythms, and repeated every 3–5 minutes. After every 2 minutes of CPR, a brief rhythm check is performed. If the rhythm transitions to VF/pVT, the VF/pVT algorithm is followed. If asystole or PEA persists, continue CPR-epinephrine cycles while actively searching for and treating reversible causes.

summary.title: Epinephrine Timing in Nonshockable Rhythms
summary.content: The 2025 guidelines emphasize early epinephrine for nonshockable rhythms. Unlike VF/pVT where epinephrine is typically given after the second shock, in asystole/PEA epinephrine should be administered as soon as an IV/IO line is established — ideally within 3–5 minutes of arrest onset. Evidence suggests that earlier epinephrine in nonshockable rhythms is associated with improved survival, particularly in witnessed arrests with a likely reversible cause.

summary.title: The H's and T's: Identifying Reversible Causes
summary.content: A systematic search for reversible causes is essential in asystole/PEA because these rhythms typically result from an identifiable, treatable underlying condition. The H's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis), Hypo-/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia. The T's: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Toxins, Thrombosis (pulmonary), Thrombosis (coronary). Each cause requires a specific diagnostic and therapeutic approach, and early identification dramatically affects the chance of ROSC.

summary.title: Confirming True Asystole with Waveform Capnography
summary.content: True asystole should be confirmed on at least two leads. Waveform capnography is invaluable: if there is no cardiac output (asystole or fine VF), ETCO₂ will be very low or absent despite adequate ventilations. An ETCO₂ <10 mmHg during CPR in a patient on an advanced airway is consistent with asystole and low cardiac output. ETCO₂ may also help distinguish pseudo-asystole artifacts from true asystole.

summary.title: PEA Subtypes and Their Treatment Implications
summary.content: PEA encompasses a spectrum — from narrow-complex organized rhythms with relatively preserved rate (which may be closest to producing a pulse) to wide-complex agonal rhythms with very slow rates. Narrow-complex PEA may suggest hypovolemia, tension pneumothorax, or cardiac tamponade as etiologies and may respond more rapidly to treatment. Wide-complex or very slow PEA may indicate severe metabolic derangement or extensive myocardial ischemia. Regardless of subtype, the algorithm is the same: CPR, epinephrine, and treat H's & T's.

summary.title: Mechanical CPR Considerations
summary.content: Routine use of mechanical CPR devices is not recommended. However, mechanical CPR (piston or load-distributing band devices) may be considered in specific circumstances where high-quality manual compressions are impractical or unsafe to sustain — such as prolonged transport, limited personnel, during ECPR cannulation, or in the catheterization lab. Performance of mechanical devices is comparable between piston and load-distributing band types.

clinicalPearls: Asystole and PEA are nonshockable — do NOT defibrillate. Focus on CPR quality and identifying reversible causes.
Give epinephrine early for nonshockable rhythms — ideally within 3–5 minutes — do not delay to establish large-bore IV access when IO is available.
Always search for the 'why' — asystole/PEA is a symptom of an underlying cause, not a final diagnosis.
Use at least two ECG leads to confirm true asystole before removing pads; waveform capnography helps confirm absent cardiac output.
redFlags: Defibrillating asystole or PEA — this wastes time and harms the patient.
Failing to systematically search for and treat reversible causes (H's & T's).
Delayed epinephrine administration while establishing peripheral IV access — use IO if IV is not quickly available.
commonMistakes: Misidentifying fine VF as asystole — always check two leads and use capnography to confirm.
Failing to search for reversible causes while repeatedly giving epinephrine — drug alone without cause-directed therapy has limited benefit.
Assuming a very slow, wide-complex PEA rhythm is asystole and stopping resuscitation prematurely.
memoryAids: 'No shock, just compressions and epi' — asystole/PEA is NOT a shockable rhythm.
H's and T's mnemonic: Hypo/Hyper (volume, O₂, K⁺, pH, temp) and the 4 T's (pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis).
Epi early for nonshockable — within 3–5 minutes — don't wait.

decisionTrees.title: Asystole / PEA Arrest Algorithm
decisionTrees.content:
Asystole or PEA identified on the monitor (confirm on at least 2 leads)
  → Start high-quality CPR immediately (compressions + ventilations)
  → Establish IV/IO access
  → Give epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO (1:10,000) as soon as feasible, ideally within 3–5 minutes
  → Continue CPR 2 min, then rhythm check
     → Still asystole/PEA: resume CPR, repeat epinephrine q3–5 min, search for H's & T's
     → Organized rhythm appears: check pulse
        → Pulse present: ROSC pathway
        → No pulse: continue as PEA
     → VF/pVT develops: switch to VF/pVT algorithm (shock)
  → Consider and treat reversible causes concurrently throughout:
     H's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion, Hypo-/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia
     T's: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins, Thrombosis (pulmonary), Thrombosis (coronary)
  → Consider waveform capnography to confirm asystole and monitor CPR quality

references: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9: Adult Advanced Life Support (Asystole/PEA management)
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Epinephrine timing in nonshockable rhythms
